perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its principles of conflict of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the People's Republic of China, without regard to its principles of conflict of laws.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to the People's Republic of China could create legal issues depending on where the agreement is executed and where the parties are located, potentially conflicting with US laws regarding jurisdiction and enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of International Law and U.S. Jurisdiction
perturbation.law_explanation: Changing the governing law to a foreign country can affect the jurisdiction of U.S. courts and the enforceability of the agreement within the U.S., potentially creating conflicts with U.S. laws regarding jurisdiction and conflict of laws under 28 U.S. Code § 1332, which addresses diversity of citizenship and alienage jurisdiction.

Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between- (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects o
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies Delaware law, while the changed text substitutes it with the law of the People's Republic of China. The Law Explanation states that changing the governing law to a foreign country affects jurisdiction within the U.S., potentially conflicting with U.S. laws. 28 U.S. Code § 1332, as referenced in the scraped snippet, addresses diversity of citizenship and alienage jurisdiction, meaning cases involving foreign entities. By changing the governing law to China, the agreement could be attempting to bypass or complicate the application of 28 U.S. Code § 1332 in US courts, creating a legal contradiction because enforceability in the U.S. becomes questionable.

perturbation.original_text: No failure or delay by either party in exercising any right, power or privilege hereunder shall operate as a waiver thereof, nor shall any single or partial exercise thereof preclude any other or further exercise thereof or the exercise of any other right, power or privilege.
perturbation.changed_text: Any delay by either party in exercising any right, power or privilege hereunder shall automatically be considered a full waiver thereof.
perturbation.explanation: This modification enforces an automatic waiver for any delay, conflicting with the common law principle that waivers must be knowing and voluntary.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text introduces an automatic waiver, contradicting common law principles of waiver that require an intentional relinquishment of a known right. This contradicts the standard for valid waivers, which typically require a knowing and voluntary action. 17 CFR § 155.3 regarding customer protection rules, while not directly on contract law waivers, demonstrates the regulatory emphasis on informed consent, which the modified waiver clause undermines.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: waiver | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you waiver A waiver is the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege . In legal contexts, a waiver is often documented in writing, but it can also be implied through actions . By waiving a right, an individual or party chooses not to enforce a particular right, claim , or provision they are legally entitled to. [Last reviewed in July of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex accidents and injuries COMMERCE commercial activities LIFE EVENTS accidents & injuries (tort law) THE LEGAL PROCESS courts criminal procedure business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure individual rights legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the common law principle that waivers must be knowing and voluntary. The original text preserved rights even with delays, while the changed text imposes an automatic waiver for any delay. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines a waiver as 'the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege.' The changed text removes the 'intentional or voluntary' aspect, creating an automatic waiver which is not legally sound.

perturbation.original_text: IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties have executed this Agreement as of the date first written above.
perturbation.changed_text: IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties have executed this Agreement but it shall not be valid or enforceable until 10 years from the date first written above.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of a 10-year delay before the contract becomes valid or enforceable is contrary to typical contract law, which assumes immediate validity upon execution, provided all elements of a valid contract are met.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaying the contract's validity by 10 years directly opposes the principle that contracts are effective upon execution, assuming all contractual requirements are satisfied.  1 U.S. Code § 2 provides rules of construction that laws go into effect immediately unless explicitly stated otherwise. Similarly, contracts are expected to be active immediately unless there is a clear and justifiable reason for a delay that does not extend so far as to void its premise.

"County" as including "parish", and so forth The word "county" includes a parish, or any other equivalent subdivision of a State or Territory of the United States. ( July 30, 1947, ch. 388, 61 Stat. 633 .)
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: effective date | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you effective date The effective date is the date on which a statute , contract , or other such legally binding instrument takes effect or becomes operative and enforceable. This date may or may not be the same date as the signing date or the date on which the agreement was executed . [Last reviewed in July of 2021 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE contracts wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 10-year delay for the contract's validity, which contradicts the general principle that contracts are effective upon execution. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that the effective date is "the date on which a statute, contract, or other such legally binding instrument takes effect or becomes operative and enforceable. This date may or may not be the same date as the signing date or the date on which the agreement was executed." While the snippet acknowledges that the effective date can differ from the signing date, a 10-year delay is an unreasonable and unusual deviation that undermines the contract's purpose. The altered text introduces an inconsistency regarding when the agreement becomes legally binding, and since the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' perturbation specifically address time-sensitive obligations the contradiction exists. Scraped Law Snippet 1 regarding 1 USC 2 is irrelevant.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior negotiations, discussions, understandings and agreements relating to such subject matter.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement does not constitute the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and is superseded by any future verbal agreements.
perturbation.explanation: This alteration invalidates the 'entire agreement' or 'integration' clause, which is intended to prevent parties from later claiming that there were other agreements beyond what's written. Making the contract subservient to future verbal agreements can create enforceability issues and uncertainty, conflicting with the Statute of Frauds in many jurisdictions.
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating the written agreement is superseded by future verbal agreements, the modification directly contradicts the Statute of Frauds (UCC § 2-201 and similar state laws), which requires certain contracts (especially those for the sale of goods over a certain value) to be in writing to be enforceable. The altered clause allows for easily disputed and potentially unenforceable alterations to the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-201. Formal Requirements; Statute of Frauds. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-201. Formal Requirements; Statute of Frauds. (1) Except as otherwise provided in this section a contract for the sale of goods for the price of $500 or more is not enforceable by way of action or defense unless there is some writing sufficient to indicate that a contract for sale has been made between the parties and signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought or by his authorized agent or broker. A writing is not insufficient because it omits or incorrectly states a term agreed upon but the contract is not enforceable under this paragraph beyond the quantity of goods shown in such writing. (2) Between merchants if within a reasonable time a writing in confirmation of the contract and sufficient against the sender is received and the party receiving it has reason to know its contents, it satisfies the requirements of subsection (1) against such party unless written notice of objection to its contents is given within 10 days after it is received. (3) A contract which does not satisfy the requirements of subsection (1) but which is valid in other respects is enforceable (a) if the goods are to be specially manufactured for the buyer and are not suitable for sale to others in the ordinary course of the seller's business and the seller, before notice of repudiation is received and under circumstances which reasonably indicate that the goods are for the buyer, has made either a substantial beginning of their manufacture or commitments for their procurement; or (b) if the party against whom enforcement is sought admits in his pleading, testimony or otherwise in court that a contract for sale was made, but the contract is not enforceable under this provision beyond the quantity of goods admitted; or (c) with respect to goods for which payment has been made and accepted or which have been received and

perturbation.justification: The altered contract explicitly states that it 'does not constitute the entire agreement' and can be superseded by 'future verbal agreements'. This directly undermines the Statute of Frauds, as explained in both the Perturbation and Law Explanations. Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 2-201(1), specifies that contracts for the sale of goods for $500 or more must be in writing to be enforceable. The modified clause creates a contradiction by allowing verbal agreements to override the written contract, making it unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds because it lacks the required written form for modifications or additional terms. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is not a valid legal text.
